---
title: "Nitrogen Shipping Cycle: Transit Model and Failure Mechanism"
subtitle: "Preliminary Investigative Report — PIR-SH-001"
author: "Opto-Mechanical Engineering Group"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
document-type: PIR
project-code: SH
status: draft
keywords:
  - nitrogen purge
  - shipping qualification
  - compliance volume
  - sub-atmospheric pressure
  - optical contamination
  - thermodynamic failure analysis
---

<!-- ============================================================
     PIR-SH-001: Nitrogen Shipping Cycle
     Investigates the transit thermodynamics of the nitrogen-purged
     optical assembly with the 2 PSIG bag vent valve as-built.

     Traceable findings use anchors: {#fnd-sh-nnn}
     Referenced downstream via: @fnd-sh-nnn
     ============================================================ -->

# Summary {#sec-summary}

This report investigates the shipping failure mode of the nitrogen-purged optical assembly. The investigation is motivated by confirmed field contamination events (moisture ingress, biological contamination) following air transport. Two lines of investigation are pursued: a helium leak check of the breather bag assembly, and a quasi-static thermodynamic model of the round-trip transit cycle. A critical unknown limits both investigations: the rigid connected volume $V_{fixed}$ (total of modules, tubing, and manifolds) has never been measured. All screening thresholds in this report are functions of $V_{fixed}$, and the production hardware cannot be located on the screening map until this measurement is completed (INV-3).

The current status of each investigation and the overall roadmap are summarized below.

::: {#tbl-roadmap tbl-colwidths="[12,30,18,40]"}
| ID | Investigation | Status | Key result |
|:---|:-------------|:-------|:-----------|
| INV-1 | Helium leak check of bag assembly | Complete | No permeation through bag material; leakage found at both ports (check valve, fill port) |
| INV-2 | Quasi-static transit model (2 PSIG vent) | In progress | Bag saturation threshold ~14 L; venting onset ~68 L; return underpressure threshold ~102 L. The 60 L nominal case does not vent under the as-built profile. |
| INV-3 | Measure $V_{fixed}$ | Not started | Required to locate the production hardware on the screening map |

Investigation roadmap. Findings from each investigation are cataloged in @sec-findings.
:::

# System Description {#sec-system}

The shipped article is a manifolded optical assembly composed of multiple sealed aluminum modules connected to a common nitrogen space through PFA interconnect tubing and intake/exhaust manifolds. The total internal nitrogen space is the sum of the module interiors, the interconnect tubing, the manifold volumes, and the shipping-time compliance bag. All of these volumes communicate freely through the manifold network and behave thermodynamically as a single connected gas volume.

In operation, a continuous nitrogen purge maintains positive gauge pressure throughout the assembly. Every joint and seal is positive-pressure energized: the seating force comes from the positive internal-to-ambient pressure differential. These seals are not qualified for sub-atmospheric internal conditions. If internal pressure falls below ambient, seating force is lost and the seal admits ambient air through the same path it normally exhausts nitrogen.

During shipping, the continuous purge is unavailable. The assembly is sealed at atmospheric pressure with a fixed initial nitrogen mass, and two passive elements manage pressure excursions across the transport cycle:

1.  **Foil compliance bag** -- a CALDRY 1500 multilayer foil reservoir that expands and contracts between approximately zero and 22 L. The foil is geometrically compliant but not elastically compliant: it unfolds and refolds between hard geometric limits with negligible restoring force across its usable range. The bag has two ports: a fill port used during initial nitrogen charge, and a check valve port holding the outward-only vent.

2.  **Outward-only check/vent valve** -- permits nitrogen to escape if internal pressure exceeds ambient by the valve's 2 PSIG cracking threshold, but does not admit ambient flow inward.

@fig-three-regimes shows the intended operating sequence across the three regimes of the round-trip cycle.

![Pressure-time regimes of the manifolded nitrogen shipping system. Top: ambient and internal pressure during the four flight phases. Bottom: schematic state of the optical modules and breather bag at each regime. Regime 1 -- bag absorbs expansion and internal pressure tracks ambient. Regime 2 -- bag reaches maximum volume and the outward-only valve vents nitrogen. Regime 3 -- return leg with bag fully collapsed and internal pressure below ambient.](figures/three-regimes.jpg){#fig-three-regimes fig-alt="Diagram showing pressure versus time across four flight phases with internal nitrogen pressure overlaid on ambient pressure. Below the curves are three schematic illustrations of an optical module with a breather bag attached, one for each failure regime. In Regime 1 the bag is partially inflated and internal pressure equals ambient. In Regime 2 the bag is fully inflated and nitrogen vents outward. In Regime 3 the bag is fully collapsed and arrows point inward at the seal locations as ambient air is drawn in."}

# Transport Envelope {#sec-envelope}

The transport state space is bounded by the shipping specification and by the ICAO standard atmosphere model used for FAA cabin altitude requirements. The relevant extremes are summarized in @tbl-transport-envelope.

::: {#tbl-transport-envelope tbl-colwidths="[35,30,35]"}
| Parameter | Value | Basis |
|:----------------------|:------------------------|:------------------------|
| Temperature range | 20 °C to 40 °C | Shipping specification |
| Maximum cabin altitude | ~8,000 ft (2,438 m) | FAA 14 CFR 25.841 |
| Ambient pressure at cabin altitude | 75.26 kPa (0.7428 atm) | ICAO standard atmosphere |
| Internal gas | Diatomic nitrogen (N~2~), ultra-pure | Purge specification |
| Maximum shipping duration | 30 to 60 days | Operational requirement |
| Bag maximum volume $V_{bag,max}$ | 22 L | CALDRY 1500 measured capacity |
| Initial bag fill $V_{bag,0}$ | ~11 L (nominal 50% fill) | Documented practice; uncontrolled |
| Bag vent cracking pressure | 2 PSIG (13.79 kPa gauge) | Valve specification |
| Rigid system volume $V_{fixed}$ | **unmeasured** | Total of modules + tubing + manifolds |

Transport envelope and key system parameters. The rigid system volume $V_{fixed}$ is the single most important unknown in the analysis.
:::

The 30 to 60 day shipping duration is significant. Even very slow inward leakage rates accumulate enough atmospheric mass over that interval to compromise nitrogen purity, particularly with respect to moisture and oxygen. The analysis that follows treats *any* sub-atmospheric internal condition as a failure, on the basis that the shipping duration is long enough to convert any quasi-static pressure deficit into a measurable contamination event.

# Field Contamination History {#sec-field-history}

::: callout-important
## Confirmed field failures motivate this analysis

Optical assemblies sealed and purged with ultra-pure nitrogen prior to shipment have returned from air transport with confirmed **moisture ingress** and **biological contamination** inside the sealed modules. Disassembly, inspection, cleaning, and re-qualification were required.

The contamination is not present at seal-up, and no operational use occurs between seal-up and post-shipment inspection. The contamination is therefore attributable to events during the transport cycle.
:::

These field failures predate the introduction of the present breather bag and check valve. The original configuration seals the manifolded assembly rigidly at atmospheric pressure with no compliance volume. During air transport the rigidly sealed system experiences the coupled pressure-temperature excursion analyzed in this document, but with no compliance reservoir and no outward relief path. Internal pressure rises and falls with the round-trip ambient and temperature cycle. On the return leg internal pressure falls below ambient, and the positive-pressure-energized seals lose seating force. Atmospheric ingress through the distributed seal network introduces moisture, oxygen, and particulates to the high-purity nitrogen space.

The breather bag and check valve concept is introduced as a passive mitigation. The intent is to provide a flexible reservoir that expands and contracts with the gas, and an outward-only valve that prevents inward flow. The mitigation addresses the original failure by providing a compliance reservoir, but it does not eliminate the underlying thermodynamic problem. As the following sections show, the mitigation can be defeated by the same coupled pressure-temperature cycle if the bag is undersized relative to the rigid connected volume.

The field history establishes two facts that the rest of the document relies on. First, the seal network is **observably permeable** to atmospheric ingress under sub-atmospheric internal conditions; this is not a hypothetical concern. Second, the failure manifests over a shipping duration measured in weeks, consistent with quasi-static thermodynamics rather than transient dynamic overpressure.

# Investigation 1: Helium Leak Check {#sec-inv-leak-check}

A helium leak check is performed on a new, unused breather bag assembly to characterize the leak integrity of the bag and its port connections independently of the transit thermodynamic model.

## Method

The bag is filled with helium and inspected with a helium sniffer detector. The inspection covers the full surface of the heat-sealed bag material, the fill port connection, and the check valve (2 PSIG outward-only vent) port connection.

## Results

- **Bag material and heat seals:** No detectable helium leakage or permeation through the bag foil or along any heat-sealed seam.
- **Check valve port:** Helium leakage detected. The port connection for the 2 PSIG outward-only check valve does not seal sufficiently.
- **Fill port:** Helium leakage detected. The fill port connection does not seal sufficiently.

Both port areas show evidence of inadequate sealing. The bag material itself is not a leakage path. Quantitative leak rates were not measured during this test; the sniffer detection establishes the existence of a leak path but not its magnitude. Bounding the leak rate is identified as a follow-up action for INV-1.

::: callout-note
## Implication for the transit model

The transit model in @sec-inv-transit assumes a perfect outward-only valve with no inward leakage. The helium leak check demonstrates that the real valve port leaks in both directions. This means the actual system is *worse* than the idealized model predicts: nitrogen can escape not only through intentional venting but also through port leakage, and ambient air can enter through the same paths during the return-leg low-pressure phase.
:::

# Investigation 2: Quasi-Static Transit Model {#sec-inv-transit}

## Failure Mechanism {#sec-mechanism}

The governing mechanism has three regimes. @fig-three-regimes shows the logic schematically, and @fig-vent-cycle shows the same behavior in the quasi-static model.

### Regime 1: Bag Absorbs Expansion

During ascent and heating, the nitrogen expands. As long as the bag has remaining headroom, the bag unfolds and internal pressure tracks ambient pressure. No harmful differential pressure develops.

### Regime 2: Bag Saturates and the Valve Vents Nitrogen

Once the bag reaches maximum volume, the system has no remaining geometric compliance. Continued expansion appears as pressure rise. When internal gauge pressure exceeds the 2 PSIG cracking threshold, the outward-only valve opens and nitrogen leaves the system. This is the irreversible step: the retained mole inventory is permanently reduced.

### Regime 3: Return Leg, Bag Collapse, and Sub-Atmospheric Pressure

During descent and cooling, ambient pressure rises and gas temperature falls. The bag collapses. If the bag reaches zero volume before the remaining nitrogen inventory can support ambient pressure in the rigid connected volume, internal pressure falls below ambient. The seal network becomes an inward leakage path and atmospheric contamination is thermodynamically favored.

::: {#fig-vent-cycle layout-ncol="2"}
![Quasi-static pressure histories for representative rigid volumes under the 2 PSIG vent. Large volumes vent nitrogen and reach negative pressure differential on the return leg.](generated/PIR-SH-001_quasistatic_pressure_examples_vent_2psig.png){fig-alt="Pressure versus time for representative rigid connected volumes under 2 PSIG outward venting. Small volumes track ambient throughout. Large volumes vent nitrogen and drop below ambient on the return leg."} ![Quasi-static bag-volume histories for the same cases. Large volumes reach full bag volume early, then collapse to zero before return to sea-level conditions.](generated/PIR-SH-001_quasistatic_bag_examples_vent_2psig.png){fig-alt="Bag volume versus time for representative rigid connected volumes under 2 PSIG outward venting. Small volumes stay within the bag range. Large volumes reach maximum bag volume and later collapse to zero."}
Representative 2 PSIG vent cases from the quasi-static model.
:::

## Governing Model {#sec-model}

The analysis uses the minimum model needed to distinguish safe compliance behavior from irreversible mass loss and return-leg underpressure.

### Assumptions

1. The working fluid is pure nitrogen (ideal gas, $\gamma = 7/5$).
2. The nitrogen is well mixed throughout the connected volume.
3. The foil bag is a geometric compliance element with negligible spring force.
4. The bag volume is clamped to $0 \le V_{bag}(t) \le V_{bag,max}$.
5. The bag valve opens at exactly 2 PSIG gauge, vents until equilibrium, and reseals perfectly. No inward flow. **Note:** The helium leak check (@sec-inv-leak-check) contradicts this assumption — both ports leak bidirectionally. The model retains the perfect-valve assumption deliberately: if the failure mechanism appears even under idealized conditions, then port leakage makes the real system strictly worse than the model predicts.
6. The shipping cycle is slow enough for a quasi-static treatment. Transient effects (rapid cabin pressure changes, thermal gradients within the gas volume) are neglected. The quasi-static envelope bounds the equilibrium states; real transient excursions cannot produce a more favorable outcome than the equilibrium prediction.
7. Seal-up occurs at 20 °C and 101.325 kPa (sea level, standard conditions). The cargo hold temperature at cruise altitude is assumed equal to the seal-up temperature (20 °C) as a nominal baseline. These are coincidentally the same value; in the parametric sweeps they vary independently.
8. The bag foil has negligible elastic stiffness. The CALDRY 1500 laminate is thin enough that restoring force across the usable volume range is orders of magnitude below the gas pressure forces. Bag stiffness does not materially affect the compliance behavior.

This model is intentionally simple. It omits bag leakage, valve hysteresis, and transient fluid dynamics. That simplicity strengthens the root-cause argument: if the mechanism appears under idealized assumptions, then bag leakage is not required to explain the observed failures.

### Governing Equations

The retained nitrogen inventory and the occupied gas volume satisfy

$$V_{tot}(t) = V_{fixed} + V_{bag}(t)$$ {#eq-vtot}

and

$$P_{int}(t)\,V_{tot}(t) = n(t)\,R\,T(t).$$ {#eq-state}

For any retained inventory, the total volume required to remain at ambient pressure is

$$V_{req}(t) = \frac{n(t)\,R\,T(t)}{P_{amb}(t)},
\qquad
V_{bag,req}(t) = V_{req}(t) - V_{fixed}.$$ {#eq-vreq}

The system resolves piecewise:

- If $0 \le V_{bag,req}(t) \le V_{bag,max}$, the bag supplies the needed compliance and $P_{int}(t) = P_{amb}(t)$.

- If $V_{bag,req}(t) > V_{bag,max}$, the bag is full and the trial pressure becomes

$$P_{trial}(t) = \frac{n(t)\,R\,T(t)}{V_{fixed} + V_{bag,max}}.$$ {#eq-ptrial}

If $P_{trial}(t)$ exceeds the vent setpoint $P_{vent,abs}(t) = P_{amb}(t) + P_{vent,g}$, the valve vents until

$$P_{int}(t) = P_{vent,abs}(t),
\qquad
n(t^+) = \frac{P_{vent,abs}(t)\,\left(V_{fixed} + V_{bag,max}\right)}{R\,T(t)}.$$ {#eq-vent-reset}

- If $V_{bag,req}(t) < 0$, the bag has collapsed and the internal pressure becomes

$$P_{int}(t) = \frac{n(t)\,R\,T(t)}{V_{fixed}}.$$ {#eq-bag-collapsed}

Ingress risk exists whenever

$$\Delta P(t) = P_{int}(t) - P_{amb}(t) < 0.$$ {#eq-delta-p}

## Closed-Form Screening Thresholds {#sec-thresholds}

The piecewise model yields closed-form screening expressions that bound the design space. Two distinct events must be distinguished: **bag saturation** (the bag reaches maximum volume) and **venting onset** (internal gauge pressure exceeds the cracking threshold after the bag is full). With a non-zero cracking pressure, the system can sustain overpressure after bag saturation without venting.

### Bag Saturation Threshold

At seal-up, the initial nitrogen inventory is

$$n_0 = \frac{P_{seal}\left(V_{fixed} + V_{bag,init}\right)}{R\,T_{seal}}.$$

At the outbound low-pressure, high-temperature peak, define the volume expansion ratio

$$\alpha =
\left(\frac{P_{seal}}{P_{low}}\right)
\left(\frac{T_{hot}}{T_{seal}}\right).$$ {#eq-alpha}

The bag avoids saturation only if

$$\alpha\left(V_{fixed} + V_{bag,init}\right)
\le
V_{fixed} + V_{bag,max},$$

which gives

$$V_{fixed}
\le
\frac{V_{bag,max} - \alpha\, V_{bag,init}}{\alpha - 1}.$$ {#eq-bag-sat}

Using the simultaneous worst-case from @tbl-transport-envelope ($P_{low} = 75.26\;\text{kPa}$, $T_{hot} = 313.15\;\text{K}$):

$$\alpha
=
\left(\frac{101.325}{75.26}\right)
\left(\frac{313.15}{293.15}\right)
= 1.438,
\qquad
V_{fixed}
\le
\frac{22 - (1.438)(11)}{1.438 - 1}
= 14.1\ \mathrm{L}.$$

Any rigid connected volume above **14.1 L** saturates the bag under the simultaneous worst-case assumption.

::: callout-note
## Bag saturation is not venting

The 14.1 L threshold is the volume at which the bag reaches maximum capacity. With a 2 PSIG cracking pressure, the system can sustain up to 13.79 kPa gauge overpressure after the bag is full without opening the valve. In the realistic 9-segment flight profile, the lowest ambient pressure (cruise) and highest temperature (tarmac) do not coincide, so the actual venting onset occurs at a substantially larger volume. The notebook simulation places the venting onset at approximately **68 L** for the nominal profile.
:::

### Venting Onset Threshold (Profile-Dependent)

With the 2 PSIG cracking pressure, the system vents only when the trial pressure after bag saturation exceeds $P_{amb} + 13.79\;\text{kPa}$. Because the worst-case temperature and worst-case ambient pressure occur at different points in the flight profile, the venting onset threshold depends on the specific temperature-altitude profile and cannot be expressed as a single closed-form number using simultaneous extremes.

The quasi-static notebook simulation (@sec-results) evaluates the full 9-segment profile and places the venting onset at approximately **68 L** for the nominal baseline (20 °C cargo, 40 °C tarmac, 8,000 ft cruise). Below 68 L, no nitrogen is lost and the system returns to seal-up conditions identically.

### Return-Leg Underpressure Threshold {#sec-return-threshold}

Once venting has occurred, the retained nitrogen inventory after venting at the 2 PSIG setpoint is

$$n_{retained}
=
\frac{P_{peak}\left(V_{fixed} + V_{bag,max}\right)}{R\,T_{peak}},$$

where $P_{peak} = P_{low} + P_{vent,g} = 75.26 + 13.79 = 89.05\;\text{kPa}$ and $T_{peak}$ is the gas temperature at the venting event.

After return, once the bag has collapsed, the internal pressure is

$$P_{return,int}
=
P_{peak}
\left(1 + \frac{V_{bag,max}}{V_{fixed}}\right)
\frac{T_{return}}{T_{peak}}.$$

To require a minimum return pressure margin $\Delta P_{req}$, define

$$\gamma(\Delta P_{req})
=
\frac{\left(P_{return,amb} + \Delta P_{req}\right)T_{peak}}
{P_{peak}\,T_{return}},$$ {#eq-gamma}

where $P_{return,amb} = 101.325\;\text{kPa}$ (return to sea level) and $T_{return} = T_{seal} = 293.15\;\text{K}$.

Then the acceptable rigid volume must satisfy

$$V_{fixed}
\le
\frac{V_{bag,max}}{\gamma(\Delta P_{req}) - 1}.$$ {#eq-return-threshold}

For the nonnegative-return case ($\Delta P_{req} = 0$) under the 2 PSIG vent, with $T_{peak} = T_{hot} = 313.15\;\text{K}$:

$$\gamma(0)
=
\frac{(101.325)(313.15)}{(89.05)(293.15)}
= 1.215,
\qquad
V_{fixed}
\le
\frac{22}{1.215 - 1}
= 102.3\ \mathrm{L}.$$

For a small positive margin ($\Delta P_{req} = 0.01\;\text{bar} = 1.0\;\text{kPa}$):

$$\gamma(0.01)
=
\frac{(102.325)(313.15)}{(89.05)(293.15)}
= 1.227,
\qquad
V_{fixed}
\le
\frac{22}{1.227 - 1}
= 96.9\ \mathrm{L}.$$

::: {#tbl-thresholds tbl-colwidths="[30,18,18,18,16]"}
| Threshold | Value | Basis | Condition | Note |
|:----------|------:|:------|:----------|:-----|
| Bag saturation | 14.1 L | Closed-form (@eq-bag-sat) | Simultaneous worst-case | Conservative bound |
| Venting onset | ~68 L | Notebook simulation | Nominal 9-segment profile | Profile-dependent |
| Return $\ge 0$ bar | ~102 L | Closed-form (@eq-return-threshold) | Simultaneous worst-case | Conservative bound |
| Return $\ge +0.01$ bar | ~97 L | Closed-form (@eq-return-threshold) | Simultaneous worst-case | With 0.01 bar margin |

Screening thresholds for the 2 PSIG vent (as-built). ICAO 75.26 kPa at 8,000 ft.
:::

## Quasi-Static Round-Trip Results {#sec-results}

@fig-min-dp-sweep shows the minimum pressure differential across the shipping cycle as a function of rigid connected volume. The differential remains at zero while the bag absorbs the outbound excursion without venting. Once venting becomes unavoidable (above ~68 L for the nominal profile), the pressure margin erodes with increasing $V_{fixed}$.

![Minimum internal-minus-ambient pressure versus rigid connected volume for the 2 PSIG vent assumption.](generated/PIR-SH-001_quasistatic_min_dp_sweep.png){#fig-min-dp-sweep fig-alt="Plot of minimum internal minus ambient pressure versus rigid connected volume. The curve stays at zero for small volumes and then declines below zero for large volumes under the 2 PSIG vent case."}

::: callout-note
## Nominal 60 L case does not vent

At $V_{fixed} = 60\;\text{L}$ with the 2 PSIG valve and the nominal flight profile, the bag fills to capacity during climb (t = 4.42 hr) but the peak overpressure reaches only 12.5 kPa — below the 13.79 kPa cracking threshold. No nitrogen is lost and the system returns to seal-up conditions identically. The system is safe at 60 L under the as-built 2 PSIG valve *if the valve and ports do not leak* (see @sec-inv-leak-check).
:::

## Multi-Cycle Accumulation {#sec-multi-cycle}

The model evaluates a single round-trip. If the system vents on any leg, the deficit is permanent: the return leg cannot restore lost nitrogen. Repeated shipping cycles ratchet the retained inventory downward. A system that survives one cycle with marginal return pressure may fail after two or three cycles as the cumulative mass loss exceeds the remaining margin.

## Cracking Pressure Paradox {#sec-paradox}

The preceding analysis might suggest that the failure is avoidable by selecting a sufficiently high cracking pressure. A higher setpoint delays or eliminates venting through the valve, which preserves the nitrogen inventory in the idealized model. However, this reasoning neglects the distributed seal network.

Any cracking pressure above zero produces a sustained positive gauge pressure inside the assembly whenever the bag is saturated. During cruise, the bag is full and the internal pressure exceeds ambient by up to the cracking threshold. This positive differential acts on every seal, joint, and port in the manifolded assembly — the same distributed network that admits atmospheric contamination under sub-atmospheric conditions. The seals are not hermetic (the field contamination history in @sec-field-history and the helium leak check in @sec-inv-leak-check both confirm this). A positive differential drives nitrogen outward through those paths for the duration of cruise.

The result is a paradox with no resolution within the bag-and-valve concept:

- **Low cracking pressure** (e.g., 0 PSIG ideal vent): the valve opens readily, nitrogen vents in bulk, and the return leg is sub-atmospheric.
- **High cracking pressure** (e.g., 7.5 PSID or higher): the valve does not open, but the sustained positive differential drives nitrogen through the distributed seal leaks for the entire cruise duration. The mass loss is slower but acts over hours. On descent, the lost mass cannot be recovered and the return leg may still be sub-atmospheric.

The cracking pressure trades the rate and path of mass loss but does not eliminate it. In both regimes, the system arrives at the return leg with less nitrogen than it started with. The only difference is whether the loss occurs through a single intentional path (the valve) or through the distributed seal network.

::: callout-note
## Quantifying the distributed leak rate

The magnitude of the distributed seal leakage under positive gauge pressure is unknown. A leak-down test — pressurizing the sealed assembly and monitoring the pressure decay — would bound this rate. Even a coarse measurement (e.g., "pressure decays to ambient within one hour at 2 PSIG") would establish whether the distributed path is consequential over a multi-hour cruise.
:::

# Findings {#sec-findings}

## From INV-1: Helium Leak Check

::: {#fnd-sh-001}
**FND-SH-001:** Helium leak testing of a new bag assembly detects no leakage or permeation through the bag foil material or any heat-sealed seam. The bag material itself is not a leakage path.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-002}
**FND-SH-002:** Helium leakage is detected at the check valve (2 PSIG vent) port. The port connection does not seal sufficiently to prevent gas transfer in either direction.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-003}
**FND-SH-003:** Helium leakage is detected at the fill port. The port connection does not seal sufficiently.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

## From INV-2: Transit Model

::: {#fnd-sh-004}
**FND-SH-004:** The bag saturates (reaches 22 L maximum volume) for any $V_{fixed}$ above 14.1 L under the simultaneous worst-case assumption. With the 2 PSIG cracking pressure, the system sustains up to 13.79 kPa gauge overpressure after saturation before the valve opens. Venting onset in the nominal flight profile occurs at approximately 68 L.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-005}
**FND-SH-005:** On the return leg, if $V_{fixed}$ exceeds approximately 102 L and the system has vented, the remaining nitrogen is insufficient to maintain ambient pressure in the rigid volume after the bag collapses. Internal pressure falls below ambient and the seal network admits atmospheric contamination.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-006}
**FND-SH-006:** The failure mechanism does not require bag leakage or valve backflow. A perfect outward-only vent and a perfect zero-stiffness bag still produce return-leg underpressure once $V_{fixed}$ exceeds the venting-onset threshold. The failure is a mass-inventory problem, not a component defect.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-007}
**FND-SH-007:** The rigid connected volume $V_{fixed}$ of the production hardware is unmeasured. Where $V_{fixed}$ falls relative to the screening thresholds in @tbl-thresholds determines whether the present concept can succeed.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-008}
**FND-SH-008:** Confirmed field failures (moisture ingress, biological contamination) predate the breather bag mitigation. The same underpressure-driven contamination appears in the original rigidly sealed concept, establishing that the seal network is observably permeable under sub-atmospheric conditions.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-009}
**FND-SH-009:** Each outbound venting event permanently reduces the retained nitrogen inventory. Repeated shipping cycles ratchet the inventory downward. A system that survives one cycle with marginal return pressure may fail after cumulative mass loss over multiple cycles.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

::: {#fnd-sh-010}
**FND-SH-010:** Increasing the valve cracking pressure does not resolve the mass-loss mechanism. Any positive gauge pressure sustained during cruise drives nitrogen outward through the distributed seal network. The cracking pressure trades the rate and path of nitrogen loss (bulk venting through the valve versus slow permeation through distributed seals) but does not eliminate it. See @sec-paradox.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

## From INV-1: Fill Port Design

::: {#fnd-sh-011}
**FND-SH-011:** The fill port joint stack-up consists of flat Teflon washers sandwiching the bag foil material, compressed by a single small-diameter fastener. The bag foil is incompressible across its thickness and does not deform to fill surface irregularities. The joint geometry is mechanically incapable of producing a face seal: no gasket-like deformation occurs regardless of applied torque. This is consistent with the helium leakage detected at the fill port in INV-1 (FND-SH-003) and constitutes a design limitation, not a workmanship issue.
*Disposition: TBD*
:::

<!-- NOTE: FND-SH-011 stack-up details are preliminary — Mike to verify
     the exact washer geometry, fastener size, and layup sequence. -->

# Conclusions {#sec-conclusions}

The transit model and the helium leak check together establish that the shipping concept has two independent vulnerability paths:

1. **Thermodynamic mass loss** -- If $V_{fixed}$ is large enough to cause venting through the 2 PSIG valve, the system loses nitrogen irreversibly on each outbound leg. The return leg cannot restore the lost mass. Under the nominal flight profile with the as-built 2 PSIG valve, venting onset occurs at approximately 68 L. Below that volume, the system completes the round trip without loss.

2. **Port leakage** -- Independent of the thermodynamic mechanism, the check valve port and fill port leak. This provides a gas transfer path in both directions that the idealized transit model does not account for. Port leakage can introduce contamination even if the bag and valve function as intended.

The immediate next step is to measure $V_{fixed}$ (INV-3) and compare the measurement against the screening thresholds in @tbl-thresholds. Depending on where $V_{fixed}$ falls, either the thermodynamic mechanism alone, the port leakage alone, or both may be responsible for the observed field failures.

<!-- ================================================================
     WORKING SECTION — Not for distribution.
     This section captures the strategic argument structure and
     remaining work plan. It will be removed or absorbed into the
     final document once the investigations close out.
     ================================================================ -->

# Working Notes: Argument Strategy and Remaining Work {#sec-working-notes .unnumbered}

::: callout-warning
## Internal working section — not for distribution

This section is a planning artifact. It outlines the argument the PIR needs to land, the escape hatches it must close, and the data still required. It will be removed before the document is issued.
:::

## Where we are

The PIR currently establishes:

1. The thermodynamic mass-loss mechanism is real and does not require any component defect (FND-SH-006).
2. The helium leak check shows both ports leak bidirectionally (FND-SH-002, FND-SH-003).
3. The fill port design is mechanically incapable of sealing (FND-SH-011).
4. Raising the cracking pressure does not resolve the problem — it trades bulk venting for distributed seal leakage (FND-SH-010, @sec-paradox).

The document is missing two things: the actual $V_{fixed}$ value and a leak rate bound.

## What the PIR needs to show

The core conclusion is: **the bag-and-valve concept has fundamental flaws that no parameter adjustment can resolve.** The audience will try to save the concept by reaching for escape hatches. Each must be closed:

| Escape hatch | How the PIR closes it |
|---|---|
| "V_fixed is small enough — it never vents" | Even below the 68 L venting threshold, the ports leak (INV-1). Over a 30–60 day shipping window, any leak path is consequential. The concept requires hermetic ports it does not have. |
| "Use a higher cracking pressure valve" | The paradox (@sec-paradox): higher cracking pressure creates sustained positive ΔP, driving N~2~ through distributed seals for the entire cruise. The system still arrives at the return leg with less nitrogen. |
| "Fix the port seals and keep the concept" | The fill port geometry cannot seal (FND-SH-011). The check valve port seats PTFE against foil — not a hermetic interface. Fixing the seals requires redesigning the port joints, at which point the concept is no longer the same concept. |
| "One trip is fine — don't reuse" | The multi-cycle ratchet (FND-SH-009) defeats this if the system is marginal. And the 30–60 day single-trip duration is long enough for port leakage to introduce contamination even without venting. |

## Remaining data to close the argument

| Item | Owner | What it proves | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{fixed}$ from CAD model (module volumes + tubing estimate) | Mike | Locates production hardware on the screening map. Determines whether the system is in the "no venting" or "venting" regime. | In progress |
| Leak-down test (pressurize sealed assembly, monitor decay) | Mike (stretch) | Bounds the distributed seal leak rate. Even a coarse result ("decays to ambient in < 1 hr at 2 PSIG") establishes the timescale. | Not started |
| Fill port stack-up verification (exact geometry, fastener size) | Mike | Strengthens FND-SH-011 with specifics | Not started |

## How the document closes out

Once $V_{fixed}$ is known:

- If $V_{fixed} < 68\;\text{L}$: the system does not vent under the nominal profile. The thermodynamic mechanism alone does not cause failure. **But** port leakage (INV-1) and the fill port design flaw (FND-SH-011) still provide independent contamination paths over the shipping duration. The concept fails on seal integrity, not thermodynamics.
- If $V_{fixed} > 68\;\text{L}$: the system vents nitrogen on the outbound leg. The thermodynamic mechanism causes return-leg underpressure independent of any seal defect. The concept fails on both thermodynamics *and* seal integrity.

Either way, the PIR concludes with a list of implied requirements for any successor design — without proposing the solution. That handoff goes to the EPS.

## Implied requirements (preview — not yet formal)

These are the requirements that the findings imply. They become formal REQ-SH-nnn items in the EPS, not here.

1. The shipping concept must conserve nitrogen mass across the full round-trip cycle, including multi-cycle use.
2. Internal gauge pressure must remain at or near zero throughout transit to avoid driving nitrogen through distributed seals.
3. All gas-boundary joints must be capable of forming a hermetic seal with the materials and geometry used.
4. The concept must tolerate the full ICAO cabin altitude range (sea level to 8,000 ft) and the specified temperature range (20–40 °C) without relying on venting.
5. The compliance mechanism must have sufficient stroke volume to absorb the thermal-barometric expansion without pressurizing the system.

# Appendix A: Bag Assembly Reference {#sec-appendix-bag}

## Known Failure Modes

@tbl-failure-modes catalogs the failure modes identified through the leak check (INV-1) and the transit model (INV-2). This table is extracted from the Rev. 3 breather bag analysis and annotated with the corresponding PIR findings.

::: {#tbl-failure-modes tbl-colwidths="[22,30,30,18]"}
| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Consequence | PIR Finding |
|:-------------|:-----------|:------------|:------------|
| Check valve stays open during ascent | Back-pressure closure incompatible with sustained pressure differential | Continuous N~2~ venting; sub-atmospheric return | FND-SH-004, FND-SH-009 |
| Check valve does not reseat reliably | CALDRY 1500 + PTFE not a hermetic seal surface | Ambient air ingress through valve port | FND-SH-002 |
| Bag compliance exceeded | Required $\Delta V$ exceeds available bag capacity | Pressure excursion beyond bag capacity; forced venting | FND-SH-004 |
| Unknown initial condition | Fill port leaks; undocumented fill volume | No repeatability between units | FND-SH-003 |
| Seal ingress on return | Positive-pressure seals not rated for sub-atmospheric conditions | Moisture, O~2~, biological contamination confirmed | FND-SH-008 |

Known failure modes of the breather bag shipping concept. Source: Breather Bag Analysis Rev. 3.
:::

## Model Symbols

::: {#tbl-symbols tbl-colwidths="[15,15,70]"}
| Symbol | Units | Definition |
|:-------|:------|:-----------|
| $V_{fixed}$ | L (or m^3^) | Total rigid connected nitrogen volume (modules + tubing + manifolds) |
| $V_{bag}$ | L | Current bag volume (0 to $V_{bag,max}$) |
| $V_{bag,max}$ | L | Maximum bag capacity (22 L for CALDRY 1500) |
| $V_{bag,init}$ | L | Bag volume at seal-up (~11 L nominal) |
| $P_{seal}$ | Pa | Atmospheric pressure at seal-up (101,325 Pa) |
| $P_{amb}$ | Pa | Ambient pressure (varies with altitude) |
| $P_{int}$ | Pa | Internal nitrogen pressure |
| $P_{vent,g}$ | Pa | Vent valve cracking pressure, gauge (13,790 Pa = 2 PSIG) |
| $T_{seal}$ | K | Temperature at seal-up (293.15 K = 20 °C) |
| $T_{hot}$ | K | Maximum temperature in transport envelope (313.15 K = 40 °C) |
| $n$ | mol | Retained nitrogen mole inventory |
| $\alpha$ | -- | Volume expansion ratio (@eq-alpha) |
| $\gamma$ | -- | Return-pressure ratio (@eq-gamma) |
| $\Delta P$ | Pa | Internal minus ambient pressure differential |

Model symbols and definitions.
:::

## Bag Assembly Configuration

The CALDRY 1500 bag assembly consists of a multilayer foil reservoir with two port connections:

1. **Fill port** -- used during initial nitrogen charge at seal-up. Intended to be sealed after filling.
2. **Check valve port** -- holds the 2 PSIG outward-only check valve. This is the only intentional gas transfer path during transit.

The foil material is a multilayer laminate designed for moisture barrier applications. The bag is heat-sealed around its perimeter. The helium leak check (INV-1) confirms that the foil material and heat seals are gas-tight; the leakage paths are at the two port connections.

<!-- TODO: Add CALDRY 1500 material datasheet excerpt, layer structure,
     and measured permeation rates if available. Add valve manufacturer
     and part number when confirmed. -->
